title: Flag manifolds over semifields II
speaker: Xuhua He
affiliation: Chinese University of Hong Kong; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: The study of totally positive matrices, i.e., matrices with positive minors, dates back to 1930s. The theory was generalised by Lusztig to arbitrary split reductive groups using canonical bases, and has significant impacts on the theory of cluster algebras, higher teichmuller theory, etc. In the first talk, we survey basics of total positivity and explain its generalization to general semifields for Kac-Moody groups. In the second talk, we shall focus on totally positive flag manifolds and discuss various topological properties.
tags: Workshop on Representation Theory and Geometry

proof_stages.label: Full flag total-nonnegative stratification
proof_stages.summary: The talk specializes to the positive real semifield, fixes a pinned minimal Kac-Moody group, defines the totally nonnegative unipotent monoids and the totally nonnegative full flag variety, and recalls its positive Richardson/Marsh-Rietsch cell decomposition.

proof_stages.label: Partial flag definitions and projected strata
proof_stages.summary: For G/P_J the totally nonnegative part is defined via projection from the full flag or by orbit closure, and the correct strata are projected Richardson varieties rather than naive intersections of Schubert and opposite Schubert cells.

proof_stages.label: Regular CW theorem as target
proof_stages.summary: The main goal is stated: under the finiteness assumption on W_J, the totally nonnegative partial flag decomposes as a regular CW complex, meaning cell closures are unions of cells and each cell closure is a closed ball.

proof_stages.label: Full-flag closure order via positive charts
proof_stages.summary: Since positive semi-algebraic pieces lack the easy field-valued transverse-intersection closure argument and arbitrary Kac-Moody groups lack finite-type duality, canonical-basis positivity is used to place cells in Bruhat open charts and factor positive Richardson cells through intermediate Weyl-group elements, yielding the closure relation.

proof_stages.label: Closed-ball property for full flag cells
proof_stages.summary: The factorization and closure relation show full-flag cell closures are compact topological manifolds with cellular boundary; induction, boundary combinatorics, and the generalized Poincare theorem then identify these closures with closed balls.

proof_stages.label: Folded Bruhat order for partial flags
proof_stages.summary: Proper projection from the full flag sends Richardson closures to unions of projected Richardson strata, so the partial-flag closure order on Q_J is obtained as a W_J-folded version of Bruhat order, which is substantially more complicated than in the full-flag case.

proof_stages.label: Atlas-map reduction to larger full flags
proof_stages.summary: Stratified atlas maps model neighborhoods in the partial flag by Schubert/Richardson strata in full flags of larger Kac-Moody groups, with enough positivity compatibility to transfer the full-flag manifold and ball arguments and assemble the regular-CW theorem.

examples.note: The usual diagonal torus, upper/lower triangular Borels, and elementary unipotent root subgroups make the abstract Kac-Moody pinning concrete, without supplying a new proof idea.

examples.name: Rank-one flag variety as a closed interval
examples.note: For the rank-one flag variety, identified with projective line, the totally nonnegative part is {0} union the positive real line union {infinity}, illustrating the Richardson cell decomposition and the closed-ball expectation.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the rank-one flag variety as P1, decomposes its totally nonnegative part into 0, positive reals, and infinity, matches these to the three Richardson cells, and identifies the result as a closed interval/closed ball.

examples.name: Coordinate refinement of a partial Richardson stratum
examples.note: A small partial-flag stratum with coordinates a1,a2,a3 splits into two projected Richardson pieces; the fact that the naive condition a1,a3>0 does not give a cell visibly motivates the projected-Richardson refinement.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly refines a partial Richardson stratum by listing the projected Richardson pieces and giving coordinate conditions on a1, a2, a3, then checks how positivity makes one piece a two-dimensional cell and another a one-dimensional cell.

passes.reason: ordinary coordinate partial-Richardson example used to show the naive unrefined totally nonnegative stratum is not a cell

examples.name: Full-flag atlas model inside G x G
examples.note: The full-flag chart decomposing as a Schubert cell times an opposite Schubert cell, equivalently a Schubert cell in the flag variety of G x G, illustrates the intended atlas-map mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the full-flag atlas model by identifying the open part with a Schubert cell times an opposite Schubert cell and then as a Schubert cell in the flag variety of the larger group G x G.
